info.parenttitle: S3.2 Functional groups: Classification of organic compounds

markdown:
## Stereoisomers – Understanding Molecules in 3D


## Cis-Trans Isomerism: A Matter of Rigidity


<definition term="Cis-trans isomerism">**Cis-trans isomerism** occurs in molecules where rotation about a bond is restricted.</definition>

1. This is most commonly observed in:
  1. **Alkenes (double bonds): **The $π$-bond in a double bond prevents free rotation.
  2. **Cycloalkanes: **The rigid ring structure locks substituents in place.
2. For cis-trans isomerism to occur, the **two substituents** attached to each side of the **double bond** (or **ring**) must be **different**. 
3. If any carbon in the double bond has **two identical substituents**, cis-trans isomerism is** not possible**.

### Recognizing Cis-Trans Isomers


1. **Cis-isomer: **Identical groups are on the same side of the double bond or ring.
2. **Trans-isomer: **Identical groups are on opposite sides.

<callout type="example">Consider but-2-ene ($C_4H_8$):

  * In the **cis-isomer**, the two methyl groups ($CH_3$) are on the same side of the double bond.
  * In the **trans-isomer**, the two methyl groups are on opposite sides.</callout>

![Structures of cis-but-2-ene and trans-but-2-ene.](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/07bf048d2adc318f46caf273ae1bc03f2868dd51-2979x1350.png)

<callout type="example">* In cycloalkanes, the reference plane is the flat face of the ring. 
* For 1,3-dichlorocyclobutane:
  * **Cis-isomer: **Both chlorine atoms are on the same side of the ring.
  * **Trans-isomer: **The chlorine atoms are on opposite sides of the ring.</callout>

![Cis-1,3-dichlorocyclobutane and trans-1,3-dichlorocyclobutane.](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/3ed792f77885ba3747363f76d8e8138155e14659-461x169.png)

<callout type="tip">Cis-trans isomerism only occurs if the molecule has** restricted rotation** and **substituents that differ on each side **of the double bond or ring.</callout>

<callout type="warning">* Students often forget that for cis-trans isomerism to occur, the groups attached to each carbon of the double bond must be different. 
* For example, propene ($CH_3-CH=CH_2$) cannot exhibit cis-trans isomerism because one carbon has **two identical hydrogen atoms**.</callout>

## Chirality and Optical Isomerism: The Mirror Image Phenomenon


### What is a Chiral Carbon?


<definition term="Chiral carbon">A **chiral carbon **(or stereocenter) is a carbon atom bonded to four different atoms or groups.</definition>

Molecules with one or more chiral carbons often exhibit optical isomerism, a type of stereoisomerism where the isomers are non-superimposable mirror images of each other, provided the molecule is chiral overall.

> These isomers are called **enantiomers**.

![Pair of mirror image enantiomers showing a chiral carbon center.](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/ccc711aac27fca7b7d6d1c7492aca7b81b92677d-2457x1125.png)

<callout type="example">#### 2-Aminopropanoic Acid (Alanine)


In alanine ($CH_3CH(NH_2)COOH$), the central carbon is chiral because it is bonded to:

* A methyl group ($CH_3$),
* An amino group ($NH_2$),
* A carboxyl group ($COOH$), and
* A hydrogen atom.</callout>

### Drawing and Identifying Enantiomers


1. Enantiomers are drawn using **wedge-dash representations **to show their 3D structure:
  1. **Wedge bond: **Points out of the plane of the paper (toward the viewer).
  2. **Dash bond: **Points behind the plane of the paper (away from the viewer).
  3. **Line bond: **Lies in the plane of the paper.
2. To draw the enantiomer of a molecule, reflect the structure across a mirror plane.

<callout type="example">#### 2-Aminopropanoic Acid


* Draw the first enantiomer with a specific arrangement of groups around the chiral carbon.
* Reflect the structure to draw the second enantiomer.

(The solution is in the image below)</callout>

![Enantiomers of 2-aminopropanoic acid with the chiral center labeled.](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/2d9b2f72b8117293a4c5ac0a8436b7083aaf3cfc-480x311.png)

### Optical Activity and Racemic Mixtures


1. Enantiomers interact differently with plane-polarized light, a property known as **optical activity**:
  1. One enantiomer rotates the plane of polarized light clockwise (**dextrorotatory**, +).
  2. The other rotates it counterclockwise (**levorotatory**, -).
2. A 50:50 mixture of two enantiomers is called a **racemic mixture**. 
3. Racemic mixtures are optically inactive because the rotations cancel each other out.

<callout type="note">In IB Chemistry (First Examination 2025), you do not have to know d/l and the +/- system by heart.</callout>

<callout type="example">* A solution of pure (+)-lactic acid rotates plane-polarized light by +3°. 
* A racemic mixture of (+)- and (-)-lactic acid will not rotate the light because the effects of the two enantiomers cancel each other out.</callout>

<callout type="tip">To distinguish between enantiomers **experimentally**, use a polarimeter to measure the direction and degree of rotation of plane-polarized light.</callout>

![Diagram showing a polarizer converting ordinary light into plane polarized light.](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/b7e5851bdbbef256563e737a55c098850232c373-2048x1020.png)



## Summary Table: Cis-Trans vs. Optical Isomerism


| Feature | Cis-Trans Isomerism | Optical Isomerism |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Key Requirement | Restricted rotation (e.g., double bond or ring) | Presence of a chiral carbon |
| Type of Isomers | Cis (same side) and Trans (opposite side) | Enantiomers (non-superimposable mirror images) |
| 3D Representation | Not required | Wedge-dash diagrams |
| Optical Activity | Not applicable | Yes |
| Example | Cis- and trans-but-2-ene | (+)- and (-)-lactic acid |
